coredata.dc:title: Engineering a zinc-rich ethyl silicate coating based on nickel oxide nanoparticles for improving anticorrosion performance

coredata.dc:description: In this research, nickel oxide (NiO) nanoparticles with different concentrations of 1, 2, 3, and 4 wt% were added to a zinc-rich ethyl silicate (ZRES) coating containing 90 wt% zinc dust particles to promote the cathodic and barrier performance of coating systems. FTIR, XRD, and EDX analysis indicated the chemical composition and created bonds in ZRES coatings. SEM, AFM and TEM images showed the surface roughness and particle size in ZRES structure. Also, TEM confirmed the uniform dispersion of NiO nanoparticles in the coating matrix loaded with 3 wt% NiO particles. The effect of NiO content and the anticorrosion behavior of nanocomposite coatings were investigated by different techniques, including open circuit potential (OCP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) and salt spray tests. Results revealed that ZRES coatings containing 3 wt% NiO nanoparticles had boosted sacrificial anode and barrier protection during 120 days of immersion in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The addition of 3 wt% NiO into the coating system significantly reduced corrosion products and blisters while increasing corrosion resistances from 3069 Ω cm2 to 16482 Ω cm2 compared with the control ZRES sample. This high-performance anticorrosion behavior of the nanocomposite coatings is mostly due to the NiO nanoparticles, which have the capability to moderate the zinc dissolution rate in addition to improving the barrier by filling porosity and creating tortuous paths.

head.ce:title: Engineering a zinc-rich ethyl silicate coating based on nickel oxide nanoparticles for improving anticorrosion performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, nickel oxide (NiO) nanoparticles with different concentrations of 1, 2, 3, and 4 wt% were added to a zinc-rich ethyl silicate (ZRES) coating containing 90 wt% zinc dust particles to promote the cathodic and barrier performance of coating systems. FTIR, XRD, and EDX analysis indicated the chemical composition and created bonds in ZRES coatings. SEM, AFM and TEM images showed the surface roughness and particle size in ZRES structure. Also, TEM confirmed the uniform dispersion of NiO nanoparticles in the coating matrix loaded with 3 wt% NiO particles. The effect of NiO content and the anticorrosion behavior of nanocomposite coatings were investigated by different techniques, including open circuit potential (OCP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) and salt spray tests. Results revealed that ZRES coatings containing 3 wt% NiO nanoparticles had boosted sacrificial anode and barrier protection during 120 days of immersion in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The addition of 3 wt% NiO into the coating system significantly reduced corrosion products and blisters while increasing corrosion resistances from 3069 Ω cm2 to 16482 Ω cm2 compared with the control ZRES sample. This high-performance anticorrosion behavior of the nanocomposite coatings is mostly due to the NiO nanoparticles, which have the capability to moderate the zinc dissolution rate in addition to improving the barrier by filling porosity and creating tortuous paths.

ce:section.ce:para: Steel materials play an important role in humans’ daily lives. They are extensively used in industry and infrastructure because of their low cost and high mechanical properties. However, these metals are susceptible to corrosion and may experience serious damage from corrosion, leading to major problems such as failure, loss of product, environmental hazards, and even tragedies [1–3]. Some practical methods for corrosion protection of steels are to utilize surface treatments [4], coatings [5], inhibitors [6], impressed current prevention [7], sacrificial anode cathodic protection [8], and hybrid technologies, which are a combination of these protection methods, like incorporating inhibitors into coatings [9,10].

Applying anticorrosion coatings to steel substrates is a cost-effective and efficient way to control corrosion. Zinc-rich coatings, one of the most common primers, have widespread application in the automotive, civil construction, and marine industries because of their high corrosion resistance capability even after mechanical damage [11–13]. In these coatings, zinc particles afford both barrier and cathodic protection due to their sacrificial action and galvanic effect. In other words, zinc-rich coatings inhibit steel's corrosion through hybrid protection. Usually, a high load of zinc particles between 80 and 90 wt% is needed to attain good protection, owing to the fact that most binders are insulators [14–17]. However, under long-term exposure, even zinc content over 90 wt% cannot ensure efficient cathodic protection due to the high rate of zinc consumption in harsh environments [18–20]. To overcome this concern, several attempts have been made to enhance the efficiency of zinc-rich coatings by adding various fillers and modifying zinc particles [21,22]. A number of researchers have discovered that the use of fillers such as clay [23], zinc oxide [24], iron waste powder [25], graphene [26,27], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [28,29], polyaniline [30], layered double hydroxide (LDH) [31], and mesoporous titanium dioxide [32] alongside zinc particles can enhance the duration of the cathodic and barrier protection.

In general, zinc-rich coatings are classified into organic and inorganic coatings, such as epoxy and ethyl silicate. According to the literature, zinc-rich ethyl silicate (ZRES) as an inorganic coating offers longer cathodic protection than zinc-rich epoxy coatings [14]. Nevertheless, the cathodic protection duration depends on many parameters in addition to the binder's chemical structure, such as zinc content, morphology, and the size of the particles [33–36]. Moreover, inorganic ethyl silicates have environmental advantages over organic silicates due to the lack of toxicity solvents. Therefore, given these benefits as well as good mechanical and weldability properties and low maintenance costs, ZRES coatings are an interesting choice as an anticorrosion coating [37].

Recently, nanomaterials have been gaining a great deal of attention due to their significant impact on industrial and basic applications especially anticorrosive nanocomposite coatings [38–40]. Among them, nickel oxide (NiO) nanoparticles are a valuable transition metal oxide material with a cubic lattice structure. Unique physiochemical, electronic, and optical properties like high chemical stability and electron transfer capability [41–43] allow NiO nanoparticles to be utilized in a broad range of applications such as magnetic materials [44], catalysts [45], battery electrodes [46], anticancer properties [47], sensors [48], and corrosion protection [49]. Yu et al. [50] reported the formulation of NiO/SiO2 nanocomposite coatings synthesized by the sol-gel process that effectively protected aluminium alloy substrates against corrosive environments. Another study conducted by El-Katori et al. [51] revealed that NiO/ZnO nanocomposite coatings can provide protection for aluminium against acid corrosion. In recent research, Ghosal et al. [52] studied the effect of NiO nanofiller anticorrosive behavior of hybrid Soy epoxy coatings. They found that with increasing NiO concentration in epoxy coating, the thermal stability, physicochemical/mechanical properties, hydrophobicity, lowering of current density were improved. Madhup et al. [53] investigated the surface morphology, anti-corrosive and abrasion resistance properties of NiO epoxy nanocomposite coatings on mild steel substrate and obtained improved behavior in the presence of nanoparticles. In similar manner, Xavier [54] found that introducing NiO nanoparticles in epoxy coatings increased the anticorrosion performance of the coating as a result of a decrease in diffusion pathways for water and oxygen.

According to previous literatures and to the best of our knowledge, no study has been reported on the effect of different concentration of NiO nanoparticles on the anticorrosive behavior of silicate coatings especially with zinc rich structures. Here, we have utilized NiO nanoparticles in the zinc rich silicate coatings with the motivation of obtaining additional barrier performance and maintaining electrical connection between zinc dust particles and the steel substrate and the zinc particles themselves. Surface methods such as SEM, EDX, AFM, TEM, FTIR and XRD were used to investigate ZRES structures. In addition corrosion techniques including impedance spectroscopy, potentiodynamic polarization and salt spray were employed.

The ethyl silicate resin was provided by Neginzereh Co. (Iran). Nickel oxide (NiO) nanoparticles with a purity of 99.8 wt% were supplied from Nia Shimi Co. (Iran). Zinc particles with an average particle diameter of 2–4 μm (superfine) were purchased from Ghatran Shimi Co. (Iran). Mild steel panels with chemical composition C: 0.15, Mn: 0.35, Si: 0.05, P: 0.005, and S: 0.005 %w (Fe: Rest) and dimensions of 20 cm × 15 cm × 0.1 cm were utilized as the substrate. The panels were cleaned with emery papers with grades 120 to 2000 and degreased with toluene and acetone.

Four types of zinc-rich ethyl silicate/NiO nanocomposites were prepared. First, the NiO nanoparticles were gradually added to the ethyl silicate resins and mixed by a mechanical stirrer with a propeller at 1200 rpm for 20 min. Next, the mixtures were sonicated using an ultrasonic UIP 1000hd (Hielscher Co.) for 30 min. During the sonication, the temperature of the mixtures was controlled up to 30 °C by placing them in an ice-water bath. Next, a proper amount of zinc dust was added to each sample and mechanically mixed again for 60 min. Finally, the films were applied to steel panels using a film applicator with a wet film thickness of 50 μm. The coatings were maintained in a cleanroom for seven days to cure and dry completely. The dry film thickness is about 35 μm. The code and composition of formulated coatings are displayed in Table 1 . All coating compositions are formulated using 10 wt% binder and 90 wt% zinc dust. The added amounts of nanoparticles were based on the wt.% of paints’ solid parts. The coating without NiO nanoparticles was prepared in the same conditions as a control sample. Fig. 1 represents a schematic diagram of the ZRES and nanocomposite coating preparation.

Open circuit potential (OCP) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted during 2880 h immersion in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution using Autolab PGSTAT 302 N in order to investigate cathodic protection, the duration of protection, and the influence of the nanoparticles in the coating systems. OCP data were daily obtained. The EIS investigation was performed with a frequency range of 100 kHz to 10 mHz and a sinusoidal voltage of 10 mV amplitude at open circuit potential. In the EIS tests, coated panels act as working electrodes, platinum is used as a counter electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) is utilized as a reference electrode. Salt spray (fog) tests model Mirab Sanat Rastin Pars were conducted under ASTM B117 in a 5 wt% NaCl spray condition at 38 °C. The photos from the samples were taken regularly up to the 28th day to examine corrosion in the scratched zone and the formation of blisters and delamination. Before testing, scratches were made manually on the coatings. A pull-off adhesion tester model 108 (Elcometer Co.) was used following the salt spray tests to measure the fabricated coating's wet adhesion strength (WAS). The test was performed according to ASTM D4541. The dispersion of NiO nanoparticles in the ZRES coating was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Zeiss EM900). To give more insight on NiO nanoparticles' morphology in the binder, an ethyl silicate coating containing NiO (without zinc particles) was also investigated via TEM. The surface morphologies of the zinc rich coatings were examined using SEMHITACHI Scanning Electron Microscopy and atomic force microscopy (AFM) model NanoSurf easyscan 2. In order to characterize the chemical structure of zinc rich coatings, XRD analysis using a Philips XRD device model with a Cu Kα radiation source and FTIR analysis using a Thermo Avatar FT-IR instrument with the KBr pellet method were performed.

Fig. 2 shows the FT-IR spectroscopy of dried zinc rich ethyl silicate loaded with 3 % nano NiO particles. In this diagram, a broad peak at 1080 cm−1 is related to the asymmetric Si–O–Si stretching vibration. Also, the peaks at 503 and 785 cm−1 are due to Si–O and Si–OH bonds, respectively [55,56] and a broad peak at 3490 cm−1 is generated by stretching vibration of –OH groups. The peak at 1460 cm−1 may be related to the C–H asymmetric/symmetric bend which can be created from residual negligible organic group in silicate structure. Moreover, the Zn–O and Zn–O–Si symmetric stretching vibration can generate peak at 560 cm−1 and a little peak at 670 cm−1 may be due to the Zn–O–Si bending vibration. The Zn–O–Si stretching or the Si–O stretching by Zn–O–Si may give infrared absorption at 870–970 cm−1, which was shielded by the strong and broad absorption of Si–O–Si stretching vibration about 1080 cm−1 [57,58].

It can be seen that no considerable peaks are observed in FTIR frequencies of zinc rich silicate nano composite related to the NiO which illustrate the physical interaction of nano nickel oxide particle with silicate matrix. The absorption band of Ni–O stretching vibration is in the region of 600–700 cm−1 which is not observed in this figure. In addition, this may be due to the low concentration and effective dispersion of NiO in zinc rich silicate matrix.

Fig. 3 presents the SEM images of zinc rich silicate coating with and without nano nickel oxide. It can be seen that the surface of silicate coatings show uniform and compact structure without crack and hole. The defects are the susceptible sites for the attack of corrosive ions and thus coatings have suitable corrosion resistance. According to this figure, the mean size of zinc particles is about 2 μm. The corresponding EDS spectrum of NiO loaded zinc rich silicate coatings is presented in Fig. 4 . According to EDS the main elements of coating are Si, Zn, O and Ni.

Fig. 5 shows the AFM images of zinc rich silicate and nano modified coatings. As illustrated in this figure, AFM results reveal a little change in surface roughness and morphology of the zinc rich silicate sample after being filled with nanoparticles. The roughness values (Sa) of zinc rich silicate coating and nanocomposite are 264.86 and 330.16 nm, respectively. In addition, no holes and porous structure are observed in AFM images which are in agreement with SEM results.

The TEM analysis was utilized to determine the exfoliation or intercalation of NiO nanoparticles in the zinc-rich ethyl silicate coating matrix. TEM images of ethyl silicate with 3 wt% NiO nanoparticles and ZRES3N are shown in Fig. 6 a and b. The bright regions of the images represent the ethyl silicate matrix. The honeycomb structure with a length distribution of 15–250 nm and a black circle are related to the cross sections of the NiO and zinc dust particles, respectively. As can be seen, the NiO nanoparticles have properly dispersed in the matrix, resulting in a decrease in the penetration of corrosive ions by creating a tortuous path and filling the porosities across the matrix. In other words, the NiO nanoparticles as a nanofiller can improve the properties of the coating. Yang et al. [59] stated that the granulated zinc powder caused several unavoidable cracks and holes in the coating. These inherent flaws can affect the cathodic protection of zinc powder and efficacy of the coating. For the ZRES3N sample, these defects could be filled with an appropriate amount of the nanofiller.

The electrochemical properties of ZRES-coated panels, including cathodic protection ability and duration, can be described via OCP measurements. Fig. 7 shows the OCP variations for coated steel substrates with ZRES containing different amounts of NiO nanoparticles during the 120-day immersion time. Based on the literature, the potential of zinc metal is approximately −1.05 V/SCE, while the steel substrate has a potential of approximately −0.65 V/SCE. Cathodic protection for steel is achieved when the potential is less than −0.86 V/SCE, according to thermodynamics [60,61]. Therefore, most studies have determined that −0.86 V/SCE is the threshold value when the equilibrium concentration of Fe2+ reaches 10−6 M [62,63]. Below this value, zinc particles (i.e., sacrificial metals), which have a lower potential, act as an anode and form a galvanic couple with the steel substrate, resulting in a mixed potential that is lower than the steel's corrosion potential, thus protecting the substrate as a cathode [64].

As illustrated in Fig. 7, it can be found that at the initial stage of immersion time, the corrosion potential of the control sample (i.e., ZRES0) and the other four nanocomposite samples decreased rapidly to near the corrosion potential of the pure zinc. This demonstrated that all coatings offer cathodic protection to the substrate in the early days of immersion. With prolonged immersion time, an increase in the potential of the samples was monitored, signifying a gradual decrease in the zinc particles' amount that were in galvanic contact with the substrate. The galvanic contact might be lost owing to the zinc particles’ corrosion, either through the formation of insulating corrosion products between the zinc-steel substrate or the complete consumption of the zinc particles [65,66].

In detail, the ZRES0, ZRES1N, ZRES2N, ZRES3N, and ZRES4N have cathodic protection durations of approximately 23, 21, 33, 56, and 21 days, respectively. The potential of the ZRES0 and ZRES4N have reached nearly the bare steel potential at the end of immersion time, suggesting the failure of protection. In contrast, the sample containing 3 wt% NiO nanoparticles had a potential of −0.78 V/SCE at the end of the 120th day. In view of Xu et al. [67] study, as long as the corrosion potential is less than −0.78 V/SCE, there is a possibility that the Fe2+ concentration is lower than 10−6 M, and no substrate corrosion occurs. These results express that the zinc particles in the ZRES3N coating had the longest cathodic and barrier protection durations, probably due to the better nanoparticles’ dispersion inside the binder that improved the shielding effect of the coating and a decrease in the zinc consumption (dissolution) rate of the coating film.

Moreover, according to the literature [68–70] and the obtained OCP data of the coatings, four main stages can be distinguished during the test period, which is demonstrated in Fig. 7.
Stage 1 (activation): The Zn particles in the coating start to work. This is shown by a drastic drop in the corrosion potential in the early part of the immersion, which reaches the same level as a zinc metal's potential. Furthermore, this shift of corrosion potential into the negative may be related to the decrease in coating porosity and the rate of electrolyte diffusion [69].
Stage 2 (cathodic protection): in this stage, a large amount of Zn particles is activated, and the corrosion potential increases to around the cathodic protection criteria of steel. The cathodic protection stage can also be characterized into three substages: growth, stabilization, and decline. In the growth substage, the zinc activation rate is significantly higher than the rate of sacrificial dissolution, and corrosion potentials continuously drop to around zinc metal. In the next substage, the zinc dissolution rate is approximately equal to their activation rate, and their potential is stabilized at a certain level. This substage is also called transition. In the last substage, the zinc consumption rate is much higher than the zinc activation rate, and the potential increases with the reduction of the zinc-steel active area.
Stage 3 (barrier protection): the corrosion potential continues to rise after the cathodic stage to the point where cathodic protection is no longer effective. In this stage, zinc oxide/hydroxide can act as a barrier and protect the coating for a long time from red rust, blistering, and peeling.
Stage 4 (failure): in this stage, corrosive ions reach the metal/coating interface and lead to significant red rust and blistering, in which the coating loses its shielding performance. The corrosion potential at this stage is usually around the steel's corrosion potential, as seen in the ZRES0 and ZRES4N samples. This stage was not observed for the ZRES1N, ZRES2N, and ZRES3N samples.

It is worth mentioning that the addition of 2 and 3 wt% of NiO particles resulted in maintaining potential values in the cathodic and barrier stages for a longer duration in comparison with the control ZRES sample. Fig. 8 schematically represents these aforementioned stages in the ZRES0 coating and ZRES with the proper amount of NiO nanoparticles.

The EIS test was used to determine the corrosion protection effectiveness of inorganic zinc-rich coatings in the presence of NiO nanoparticles. The test was performed at various immersion times up to 120 days for different prepared samples. The results are shown as Nyquist, phase and Bode plots in Figs. 9 and 10
and 12, respectively.

Fig. 9 displays Nyquist diagrams of prepared samples after 1, 8, 18, 50, 87, and 120 days of exposure to a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The semicircle radius of the samples had a remarkable increase after 8–18 days of immersion, indicating corrosive ions diffused into zinc coatings, leading to the formation of zinc oxide in defect areas. Particularly in the case of ZRES3N, the semicircle radius at day 18 expanded beyond that of day one. This high protection behavior can be attributed to the passivation product formed by Zn and barrier NiO particles. In the following immersion time, the semicircle's diameter increased, corresponding to a decrease in cathodic protection and an increase in barrier protection for the samples. Then, the diameter of the semicircle experienced a decrease, demonstrating a decrease in barrier protection proficiency. This is due to the corrosive ions and water penetration through the coating and the decreasing protection. According to phase diagrams (Fig. 10), impedance diagrams include two depressed arcs which are overlapped. The first arc in high frequency region is related to parallel combination of double layer capacitance and charge transfer resistance for zinc oxidation reaction in coating surface. The second arc in low frequencies is related to zinc rich silicate coating resistance and capacitance. The electrical equivalent circuit suitable with the impedance plots is shown in Fig. 11 . The equivalent circuit includes Rct, Qdl and Rs, which represent the charge transfer resistance of Zn oxidation reaction, a constant phase element of the double layer capacitance and solution resistance. In addition, Rc and Qc are corresponded to the resistance and capacitance of zinc rich silicate coatings. It is necessary to change the capacitor (C) with a constant phase element (CPE) Q in the equivalent circuit for obtaining suitable impedance simulation. The presence of depressed loops and CPE behavior in impedance data is related to microscopic roughness which causes an inhomogeneous distribution in the double-layer capacitance and also in the solution resistance [71,72]. The simple theoretical transfer function Z(ω) for equivalent circuit is:

Where ω is the frequency in rad/s, ω = 2πf and f is frequency in Hz. To corroborate the equivalent circuit, the experimental data are fitted to equivalent circuit and the circuit elements are obtained. Table 2 indicates the impedance data for corrosion of zinc rich silicate coating. According to these results, with increasing immersion time, zinc dissolution resistance increases due to the consuming zinc particles and increasing zinc oxide. Moreover, the coating resistance increase and the coating capacitance reduces with enhancing immersion time which is related to the increase and accumulation of zinc oxide in coating pores, and it would decrease active area of the substrate. In long immersion time about 120 days, the coating resistance decreases due to the penetration of water and corrosive ions beneath the coating layer, which leads to coating degradation and steel corrosion.

For the Bode plots in Fig. 12 , the value of impedance at low frequencies represented the shielding performance of coatings; the higher value related to better protection [73]. At initial immersion time (day 1), the impedance (Log |Z|) value of the ZPES0 was 2.63 Ω cm 2, whereas other coating samples containing NiO nanoparticles showed higher Log |Z| values of about 2.8–3 Ω cm 2. At day 8, the impedance value of all samples increased to some extent, indicating the anode reaction had begun (i.e., Zn anode-Fe cathode). This increment trend of the impedance values for the ZPES1N, ZPES2N, and ZPES3N continued with a prolonged immersion time of up to 87 days, suggesting that zinc oxide products could fill the porosity of coatings and enhance their protection performance. In contrast, the upward trend of the impedance values for the ZPES0, and ZPES4N lasts for 50 days. After these times (50–87 days), the impedance values gradually decreased, showing a decrease in the coatings’ protection effectiveness. At the final immersion time, the corrosion resistance values of the ZPES0, ZPES1N, ZPES2N, ZPES3N, and ZPES4N were approximately 3069, 9332, 13084, 16482, and 6918 Ω cm 2, respectively. In brief, ZRES with 3 wt% NiO nanoparticles revealed a higher impedance value and cathodic protection capability than other samples.

For better comparison, the evolution of impedance at low frequencies of the coating samples is displayed in Fig. 13 . In the first period, all samples' impedance decreases as immersion time increases, owing to the water diffusion and the zinc particles' activation [23]. Afterwards, the impedance increased due to the zinc particles' oxidation, which can fill the coating porosities. Meanwhile, the nanocomposite samples show an increment in the impedance values with respect to the ZRES0 sample. This behavior may be because of the better barrier properties of these coatings, which resulted from the combined effect of activated zinc and NiO nanoparticles, which forces corrosive ions to traverse a longer path to reach the steel. In the final period, resistance decreases due to the dissolution of iron or a decrease in coating shielding. During the whole process, the highest Log|Z|0.01Hz value of ZRES0 showed up at the 38th day with 4.1 Ω cm 2, while the highest Log|Z|0.01Hz value of ZRES3N showed up at the 50th day with 4.52 Ω cm 2, confirming that ZRES3N achieves better barrier performance than ZRES0. A higher value of ZRES resistance for NiO-modified ZRES can also indicate a decrease in the coating's porosity [74–76].

Based on Figs. 9–13, the ZRES0 and ZRES4N had the least cathodic protection time, while the ZRES3N exhibited a longer cathodic protection time and had the highest impedance value. In other words, the cathodic protection duration was improved with increasing NiO nanoparticle concentrations up to 3 wt%. However, increasing NiO nanoparticle concentration to more than 3 wt% resulted in a decrease in the impedance value, which could relate to the NiO agglomeration. The increase in cathodic protection duration for ZRES coatings containing 3 wt% NiO nanoparticles can be ascribed to the efficient dispersion of the particles inside the ethyl silicate, which thus reduces corrosive ions diffusion into the sample by improving its barrier properties. The underperformance of the ZRES0 coating compared to the nanocomposite coatings is consistent with Bastos et al. work [77]. They chemically modified the surface of zinc dust particles with organic and inorganic inhibitors and investigated modified coatings through the scanning vibrating electrode technique. Their outcomes revealed that all the modified samples exhibited performance improvements resulting from a decrease in the reactivity of Zn particles. In summary, the experimental results of the EIS plots clearly indicated that the anticorrosion ability of the samples followed the trend ZRES3N > ZRES2N > ZRES1N > ZRES4N > ZRES0. This performance was in line with the OCP tests (Fig. 7).

The potentiodynamic polarization curves of zinc rich silicate coatings after 30 days immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution are illustrated in Fig. 14 . Zinc rich coatings include different amount of nano NiO particles. Corrosion parameters were listed in Table 3 . As seen, in the presence of nano NiO, corrosion current reduces and corrosion potential shifts to more negative potentials. Maximum corrosion resistance is obtained for the nano composite sample including 3 % NiO which is in agreement with measured impedance data.

Apart from the electrochemical investigation, the salt spray test was conducted as an accelerated corrosion testing technique to analyze the anticorrosion behavior of the samples further [78,79]. The images of the samples during the exposure to the salt spray condition are depicted in Fig. 15 . According to ASTM D714-02, the samples’ blister size and frequency (density) are summarized in Table 4 . The blister sizes were identified by numbers ranging from 0 to 10, with 0 bringing the biggest size blister and 10 representing no blistering. A series of letters following the number of blisters indicated their frequency of occurrence. The blisters with the highest frequency are denoted with the letter D (dense), while less dense blisters are denoted with the letters MD (medium dense), M (medium), and F (few). Some examples of the blisters are shown with a yellow circle in Fig. 15.

Overall, in the initial exposure time, Zn particles provide cathodic protection (as a sacrificial anode), and with prolonged exposure time, Zn particles offer barrier protection due to the formation of zinc corrosion products that can seal the coating [14]. It can be seen that the cathodic protection duration of ZRES0 significantly changed with the addition of NiO nanoparticles. Regarding the ZRES0, some blisters were detected after 12 days of exposure to the salt spray condition, and red rust appeared around the scratched zone after 14 days, showing the coating had no longer provided effective protection. With increasing exposure time, the coating sample experienced more degradation, and the number of blisters and rust increased. The diffusion of corrosive ions like O2, Cl−, and H2O into the coating/substrate interface is responsible for making the coating blister, weakening the adhesion bond's stability, and accelerating the coating's degradation. In terms of the nanocomposite coatings, ZRES1N, ZRES2N, and ZRES4N coatings exhibited blisters after 12 days, while no evidence of blisters was observed in the ZRES3N sample even after 24 days, indicating excellent inhibition of corrosive ion diffusion. Moreover, red rust was detected around the scratched zones of the ZRES1N and ZRES4N coatings after 14 days. At this stage, the substrate is under corrosive agent attack, which means that the coating has lost all its protective performance. However, no red rust was identified at the ZRES2N and ZRES3N coatings, signifying the samples are still under cathodic and barrier protection, although the ZRES3N exhibited the production of a layer of zinc hydroxide/oxide on the surface. Altogether, the ZRES sample containing 3 wt% NiO nanoparticles had comparatively improved cathodic protection performance in the salt spray condition concerning the ZRES0 sample by showing no obvious red rust or blisters.

Fig. 16 illustrates the X-ray diffraction analysis of zinc rich silicate coating in the range 10°< 2θ < 90° after 28 days of exposure to a salt spray chamber. Remained zinc metal and corrosion products such as ZnO, Zn2SiO4 and Zn5(OH)8Cl2.H2O characteristic peaks are distinguished from XRD analysis [57,80,81]. Because the silicate layer is thin and amorphous, characterization of silicate matrix with normal XRD is too difficult.

Conventional zinc-rich coatings, generally, because of high-loading zinc particles, suffer from weak adhesion. In this regard, the adhesion properties of different ZRES coatings after 28 days of exposure to the salt spray condition were investigated with the pull-off tests [82,83], and the outcomes are shown in Table 5 . Although the WAS values of all samples were close to each other, results could reveal that adding NiO nanoparticles had no adverse effect on the ZRES coating's WAS. The ZRES3N sample had the highest WAS of 4.87 MPa, while ZRES0 had a WAS of 4.71 MPa. In terms of the nanocomposite coatings, the penetration of corrosive ions decreased with the addition of NiO nanoparticles. Therefore, the lower value of WAS in the ZRES0 may be a result of more aggressive ions penetrating the coating, which can cause the coating to lose adhesion to the substrate and degrade other properties. These findings are in accordance with the electrochemical and salt spray tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanisms for nanocomposite coating corrosion protection
ce:section.ce:para: In the ZRES coating, as discussed, corrosive ions can diffuse through the ineluctable holes and cracks in the coating, causing deterioration and oxidation at the interface between the coating and steel, which lead to severe corrosion of the substrate. This behavior may be linked to the early consumption of zinc particles and the limited barrier properties formed by the zinc corrosion products, as revealed by OCP, EIS, and salt spray tests. By adding NiO nanoparticles to the ZRES coating, the synergy of zinc particles and NiO nanoparticles creates better barrier properties and suitable electrically conductive networks, which allow zinc particles to continue electrical contact with the substrate and themselves. NiO nanoparticles are inorganic material which don't react with corrosive ions and can fill defects and atomic holes and thus block diffusion paths of these ions. Usually, these nano particles have very low density and therefore nano particles produce high volume and can disperse effectively in coating structure. In other words, NiO nanoparticles improve the coating's compactness and create tortuous path for preventing the diffusion of corrosive ions to the substrate and thus lead to decreasing zinc consumption and balancing the generation of zinc corrosion products. As the concentration of NiO nanoparticles increases, they provide a better compactness for preventing the diffusion of corrosive ions and longer cathodic protection, as illustrated in Figs. 7 and 13. It seems that with 3 % nano NiO, the maximum matrix filling with suitable zinc electrical connection is achieved which leads to optimum corrosion protection. However, when the concentration of NiO nanoparticles is too high (i.e., ZRES4N), it can be proposed that zinc particles separate further from each other, and it is hard to be connected with others; therefore, the number of activated zinc particles participated in cathodic protection dramatically decreases compared to ZRES3N. 1%w NiO produce high volume due to its low density and thus in comparing 3 % and 4%w NiO, the difference in dispersed volume of nanoparticles is considerable and effective. Moreover, high amounts of NiO nanoparticles can agglomerate and create porosity in the ethyl silicate coating, which can reduce efficient electron transport pathways. Consequently, the appropriate amounts of nanoparticles can improve the corrosion resistance of the ZRES to some level. And the better corrosion protection of ZRES3N can be attributed to the stronger anode sacrificial and barrier protection provided by the combination of NiO and zinc particles. Fig. 17 schematically illustrates anticorrosion mechanisms for a ZRES coating containing the proper amount of NiO nanoparticles.

This research investigated the effect of incorporating different amounts of NiO nanoparticles on the cathodic and barrier protection of ZRES coatings in a saline solution. The results of the anticorrosion behavior of the nanocomposite zinc-rich coatings were compared with the ZRES coating without the nanoparticles. The following noteworthy discoveries were made:
1
OCP results indicated the superior protection performance of nanocomposite coatings when the amount of NiO nanoparticles in the coating is 3 wt%. It maintains the cathodic protection ability for a longer duration, while the unmodified coating loses its cathodic protection in a shorter time, which is related to the high rate of zinc consumption and lack of effective barrier properties.
2
EIS results confirmed proper cathodic and barrier protection of the ZRES samples loaded with 3 wt% NiO nanoparticles after long-term exposure. However, adding more than 3 wt% NiO nanoparticles showed a decrease in the protection time of the sample due to the agglomeration of NiO, which leads to increased porosity in the coating. The findings imply that the proper amount of NiO can be a good modifier for ZRES and improve protection service life.
3
Salt spray test outcomes demonstrated the formation of blisters and red rust at the unmodified sample. However, adding 3 wt% NiO nanoparticles in the ZRES improved the anticorrosion properties of the zinc-rich coating significantly, which showed no sign of blisters or red rust.
4
The anticorrosion performance improved as the NiO nanoparticles increased to 3 wt%. This contributed to an additional barrier effect by filling porosities, improving zinc electrical contact, and balancing zinc consumption by combining zinc dust and NiO particles.

Thus, herein is introduced a facile method for the preparation of a high-performance ethyl silicate zinc-rich nanocomposite coating that possesses broad applicability potential in the field of metal protection.

Hanieh Salehinasab: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Rezvan Majidi: Visualization, Writing – original draft. Iman Danaee: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Ladislav Vrsalović: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Salman Saliminasab: Investigation, Methodology, Validation. Davood Zarei: Methodology, Resources.
